key_insight: A dual-domain ResNet18 can classify crystallographic surface handedness from either atomic geometry or reciprocal-space Fermi surface projections, with much stronger performance in momentum space.
engineering_relevance: This enables practical chirality labeling for catalytic, sensing, and spintronic surface design, and suggests momentum-resolved measurements are a more reliable operational signal than local atomic geometry.
method: Fine-tuned ResNet18 on labeled atomic-structure images and simulated Fermi surface projection images.
Compared real-space versus reciprocal-space representations for surface chirality classification.
Transferred the reciprocal-space model to synchrotron experimental images using minimal fine-tuning.
evidence_signal: The model achieved about 73% accuracy on atomic models and about 99% on Fermi surface projections, and the experimental transfer worked after fine-tuning on just two labeled frames.
watch_item: The experimental transfer result is promising but appears to rely on very limited fine-tuning data, so robustness across broader materials, noise levels, and measurement conditions still needs validation.
paper_title: Decoding Crystallographic Surface Chirality with Machine Learning: From Atomic Geometry to Fermi Surface Projections
